did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a weakly supervised, end-to-end AI system combining a pretrained pathology foundation model (UNI encoder) with gated attention-based multiple instance learning (MIL) for the classification of cutaneous T-cell lymphomas (CTCL) and cutaneous lymphoproliferative disorders (CLPD) using H&E slides. Additionally, a large language model (GPT-4o) was used for automated cohort curation and label generation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is identified as a 'POSTER' abstract from 'The 67th ASH Annual Meeting Abstracts' (Blood 146, 2025). It presents original experimental results, including the development of a weakly supervised AI system, specific methodology (LLM-assisted cohort curation, UNI encoder, gated attention MIL), and quantitative performance metrics (AUROC 0.96, accuracy 0.84) on a held-out test set. Per the instructions, conference abstracts with original results are classified as Research papers.

title: AI-based diagnosis of cutaneous lymphoma and lymphoproliferative disorders via H&E morphology and LLM-assisted cohort curation
brief_summary: This study developed a weakly supervised AI system to classify cutaneous T-cell lymphomas (CTCL) and cutaneous lymphoproliferative disorders (CLPD) using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) slides. The model utilized a pretrained UNI pathology foundation encoder combined with gated attention-based multiple instance learning for binary classification. Large-scale training data was generated by applying the GPT-4o large language model to 2,803 pathology reports from Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, resulting in 2,493 whole-slide images. Evaluation was performed on a held-out test set of 50 slides verified by a dermatopathologist. The model achieved an AUROC of 0.96, an overall accuracy of 0.84, and a specificity of 100%. The findings suggest that combining LLM-assisted cohort curation with computer vision can effectively diagnose challenging histopathologic conditions based on H&E morphology alone.

lesion_types_studied.details: Positive cases in the binary classification task; 1,011 positive cases identified via LLM-assisted cohort curation from 2,803 pathology reports, corresponding to 2,493 whole-slide images used for training. Evaluated on a held-out test set of 25 positive slides.
Grouped with CTCL as the positive class for the binary classification task. The model distinguishes these clonal lymphoproliferations from reactive mimics.

Negative cases in the binary classification task; 1,482 negative cases identified via LLM-assisted cohort curation. These are clinically and pathologically masquerading conditions that the model distinguishes from CTCL/CLPD.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of cutaneous T-cell lymphomas (CTCL) and cutaneous lymphoproliferative disorders (CLPD) versus reactive inflammatory dermatoses (mimics).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the development of a system for 'binary classification' of CTCL/CLPD using H&E slides. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (positive vs. negative for CTCL/CLPD) using a pretrained UNI pathology foundation encoder and gated attention-based multiple instance learning (MIL). Performance was evaluated using AUROC (0.96), accuracy (0.84), sensitivity (66.7%), specificity (100%), and precision (1.00).

datasets_used.name: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (MSKCC) Cohort
datasets_used.description: A retrospective cohort of pathology reports and corresponding whole-slide images from Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center. The cohort was curated using GPT-4o to parse 2,803 pathology reports, identifying cases with CTCL/CLPD or reactive mimics. The dataset consists of H&E stained whole-slide images.

use.training: Used for model training. 2,493 whole-slide images were initially identified via LLM-assisted cohort curation. One positive case was excluded from the test set due to inconclusive diagnosis, leaving 2,443 images for training (2,493 total - 50 test).
use.testing: Used for evaluation. A balanced, held-out test set of 50 slides (25 positive, 25 negative) was randomly selected from the LLM-labeled dataset and verified by a dermatopathologist.
use.other: The LLM (GPT-4o) was used for automated cohort curation and label generation from 2,803 pathology reports.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper reports descriptive performance metrics (AUROC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision) without statistical significance tests or p-values.

Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (.description: Balanced test set of 50 slides (25 positive, 25 negative) randomly selected from the LLM-labeled dataset and verified by a dermatopathologist. One case initially labeled as CTCL was excluded due to inconclusive diagnosis.
Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (.notes: The model demonstrated strong performance in distinguishing cutaneous lymphoproliferative disorders from reactive mimics using H&E morphology alone. Precision of 1.00 indicates perfect positive predictive value. Attention maps revealed strong localization to perivascular and lichenoid infiltrates, intraepidermal/epidermotropic regions, and adnexal structures.

architecture_used.main_model: Weakly supervised, end-to-end AI system combining a pretrained pathology foundation model (UNI encoder) with gated attention-based multiple instance learning (MIL)

training_details.training_data_source: LLM-assisted cohort curation (GPT-4o) of 2,803 pathology reports from Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center

classification_or_output_head.type: Gated attention-based multiple instance learning aggregation for binary classification

preprocessing_techniques: Patch extraction: 256x256 patches extracted from whole-slide images at 20x magnification
Feature extraction: 1,024-dimensional embeddings extracted using the UNI pathology foundation encoder
conclusions: The study demonstrates the synergistic application of large language models (GPT-4o) for automated cohort curation and advanced computer vision techniques to train high-performing models for challenging histopathologic diagnoses.

A weakly supervised AI system combining a pretrained UNI pathology foundation encoder with gated attention-based multiple instance learning achieved highly accurate diagnosis of cutaneous lymphoproliferative disorders based on H&E morphology alone.
The model achieved an AUROC of 0.96, overall accuracy of 0.84, sensitivity of 66.7%, and specificity of 100% on a dermatopathologist-verified held-out test set.
The model's precision of 1.00 indicates it is highly reliable for confirming the presence of cutaneous lymphoproliferative disorders, although it has moderate sensitivity.

Attention maps revealed strong localization to clinically relevant features, including perivascular and lichenoid infiltrates, intraepidermal/epidermotropic regions, and adnexal structures, highlighting features learned without explicit supervision.
The AI-based approach to CTCL shows promise for reducing diagnostic variability and improving triage.
important_remarks: The study is a conference abstract (ASH 2025) rather than a full peer-reviewed paper, limiting the depth of methodological detail and statistical reporting.
Training labels were generated automatically using GPT-4o on pathology reports, introducing potential label noise or bias inherent to LLM interpretation of clinical text.

The model was trained and evaluated on a single-institution dataset (Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center), raising concerns about external validity and generalizability to other populations or scanners.
The held-out test set was very small (n=50), which may lead to unstable performance estimates and overfitting to the specific test cases.
One positive case was excluded from the test set due to an 'inconclusive diagnosis' after initial LLM labeling, indicating potential ambiguity in the ground truth or LLM labeling process.

The model exhibits high specificity (100%) and precision (1.00) but moderate sensitivity (66.7%), meaning it is reliable for confirming disease but misses a significant portion of true positive cases.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported for the performance metrics.
The demographic distribution of the training data is partially reported (47% female, mean age 61 ± 15 years), but racial/ethnic diversity is not mentioned, posing a risk for demographic bias.
